agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a tiered gateway architecture with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal) sharing a common gateway core but with channel-specific policies. This eliminates duplicated auth logic from downstream services, centralizes rate limiting, and enforces network-level isolation for internal traffic. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with route-based policy selection rather than three separate gateways, balancing operational simplicity against clean separation of concerns.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic (mobile + partner). Handles TLS termination, request routing, authentication dispatch, rate limiting enforcement, request/response transformation, and observability (access logs, metrics, tracing headers). Runs as a horizontally-scaled stateless cluster behind a load balancer.","boundaries":"Owns no business logic. Never calls databases directly. Only concerns: identity verification, policy enforcement, routing, and observability. Delegates auth token validation to the Auth Service."},{"name":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","responsibility":"Lightweight sidecar or internal-only ingress that routes service-to-service traffic. No authentication but enforces mTLS for transport security, provides circuit breaking, retries, and observability. Listens on a private network interface only.","boundaries":"Accessible only from the internal VPC/network. No public IP. No external DNS. Enforced via network policy (security groups or Kubernetes NetworkPolicy)."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Centralized authentication and identity resolution. Validates JWT tokens (mobile), validates API keys (partner), and returns a canonical identity envelope (principal ID, consumer type, scopes/permissions). Stateless validation with a shared signing key or JWKS endpoint.","boundaries":"Does not authorize — only authenticates and enriches. Authorization decisions remain with downstream services that understand their own domain rules."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Enforces per-consumer rate limits using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) on every response. Supports tiered limits: 100 req/min per mobile user, 1000 req/min per partner API key.","boundaries":"Shared Redis cluster for distributed counting. Gateway calls rate limiter as a pre-route filter. If Redis is unavailable, fails open with degraded logging (availability over correctness for rate limiting)."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages partner webhook subscriptions, serializes domain events into webhook payloads, delivers with exponential backoff retry (max 5 attempts), and tracks delivery status. Partners register endpoints via the Partner API.","boundaries":"Consumes domain events from an internal message broker (e.g., SQS, Kafka). Does not poll services. Isolated from the request path — purely async outbound."},{"name":"Downstream Services","responsibility":"Business logic services (orders, tracking, inventory, etc.). After gateway migration, these services no longer handle auth or rate limiting. They receive a trusted X-Consumer-Identity header from the gateway and make local authorization decisions.","boundaries":"Trust the gateway's identity header only when the request arrives from the internal network. Must reject requests with identity headers that arrive on any external interface (defense in depth)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT token in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry via Auth Service, extracts user_id, enforces 100 req/min rate limit keyed on user_id, then proxies to downstream with X-Consumer-Identity header.","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (HTTPS/REST)"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with API key in X-API-Key header. Gateway validates key via Auth Service, resolves partner_id and scopes, enforces 1000 req/min rate limit keyed on partner_id, then proxies to downstream with X-Consumer-Identity header including scopes.","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (HTTPS/REST)"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","contract":"mTLS over private network. No auth token required. Service identity derived from mTLS certificate CN. Internal gateway adds X-Consumer-Identity with service name and type=internal.","coupling_type":"platform coupling (network + mTLS)"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"gRPC call: ValidateToken(token, type) → IdentityEnvelope{principal_id, consumer_type, scopes[], expiry}. Sub-5ms p99 latency target. Gateway caches successful validations for 60s keyed on token hash.","coupling_type":"contract coupling (gRPC schema)"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"In-process filter or sidecar call: CheckRate(principal_id, consumer_type) → {allowed: bool, remaining: int, reset_at: timestamp}. Redis-backed sliding window.","coupling_type":"operational coupling (shared Redis)"},{"from":"Downstream Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker topic (e.g., order.shipped, tracking.updated). Webhook Dispatcher consumes events matching partner subscriptions and delivers via HTTPS POST with HMAC signature.","coupling_type":"event coupling (async, message broker)"}],"data_flow":"1. External request hits load balancer → API Gateway (Edge). 2. Gateway extracts credentials (JWT or API key) and calls Auth Service for validation. 3. On auth success, gateway calls Rate Limiter with resolved principal. 4. If under limit, gateway attaches X-Consumer-Identity header and proxies to downstream service. 5. Downstream service reads identity header, performs domain-level authorization, processes request, returns response. 6. Gateway strips internal headers from response, adds rate limit headers, returns to client. 7. For webhooks: downstream services emit domain events → message broker → Webhook Dispatcher → partner endpoints. 8. Internal service-to-service: requests go through Internal Gateway on private network with mTLS, bypassing Edge Gateway entirely.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) that were inconsistently duplicated across services. Single enforcement point eliminates the class of bugs where one service has weaker auth than another."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Services can be migrated incrementally. Gateway routes to existing services unchanged — the only difference is auth/rate-limiting moves to the gateway. No big-bang rewrite required."},{"pattern":"Token Relay with Identity Propagation","rationale":"Gateway validates credentials and converts them into a canonical internal identity envelope. Downstream services never see raw tokens or API keys — they get a trusted, normalized identity. This decouples internal services from auth mechanism changes."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed-window counters (avoids burst at window boundaries). Redis-backed for distributed counting across gateway instances. Fails open to preserve availability."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker (Internal Gateway)","rationale":"Prevents cascade failures in service-to-service communication. Internal gateway applies circuit breaking per upstream service."},{"pattern":"Async Webhook Delivery with Retry","rationale":"Decouples webhook delivery from the request path. Exponential backoff prevents thundering herd on partner endpoint recovery."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"API Access Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["ConsumerIdentity (entity: principal_id, type, scopes, rate_limit_tier)","APIKey (entity: key_hash, partner_id, scopes, active, created_at)","RateLimitPolicy (value object: tier, max_requests, window_seconds)"]},{"name":"Webhook Delivery","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription (entity: partner_id, event_types[], endpoint_url, secret, active)","DeliveryAttempt (entity: subscription_id, event_id, status, attempts, next_retry_at)"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Tracking — these are the actual business domain aggregates, unchanged by gateway introduction"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Delivery","relationship":"Customer-Supplier: Logistics Core publishes domain events. Webhook Delivery subscribes and transforms them into partner-facing payloads. Logistics Core does not know about webhooks."},{"upstream":"API Access Management","downstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","relationship":"Open Host Service: Auth Service exposes a gRPC API for identity validation. Gateway is a consumer. Auth Service defines the contract."},{"upstream":"API Access Management","downstream":"Webhook Delivery","relationship":"Conformist: Webhook Delivery uses partner_id from API Access Management's identity model to match subscriptions. It conforms to the identity model without translating."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single gateway deployment with route-based policy selection instead of separate gateways per consumer type","gains":"Lower operational overhead — one deployment to monitor, scale, and patch. Shared infrastructure cost. Simpler DNS and certificate management.","sacrifices":"Blast radius: a gateway misconfiguration or overload affects all consumer types simultaneously. Policy complexity lives in route configuration rather than deployment topology.","alternatives_considered":["Three separate gateway deployments (one per consumer type) — cleaner isolation but 3x operational burden and infrastructure cost","BFF pattern with separate backends — over-engineered for current scale, better suited when mobile and partner APIs diverge significantly in shape"]},{"decision":"Centralized Auth Service rather than gateway-embedded auth logic","gains":"Auth logic is testable and deployable independently. Can evolve auth mechanisms (add OAuth2, SAML) without redeploying gateway. Single source of truth for key/token validation.","sacrifices":"Added network hop for every request (mitigated by 60s cache). Auth Service becomes a critical dependency — requires high availability.","alternatives_considered":["Embed JWT validation directly in gateway using JWKS — faster but couples gateway to auth mechanism, and API key validation still needs a service call","Sidecar auth proxy — adds deployment complexity without meaningful benefit over a shared service"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed sliding window rate limiter with fail-open policy","gains":"Accurate distributed rate limiting across gateway instances. Fail-open preserves availability during Redis outages — rate limiting is a protective measure, not a security boundary.","sacrifices":"During Redis outages, rate limits are not enforced. Malicious actors could exploit the window. Redis adds an infrastructure dependency.","alternatives_considered":["Local in-memory rate limiting per gateway instance — simpler but inaccurate when scaled horizontally (each instance sees only its own traffic)","Fail-closed rate limiting — safer but a Redis outage would cause a full platform outage, unacceptable for a logistics platform"]},{"decision":"mTLS for internal gateway instead of no security","gains":"Defense in depth. Even if network isolation is breached, services cannot be impersonated. Service identity is cryptographically verified.","sacrifices":"Certificate management overhead. Requires PKI infrastructure (or a service mesh like Istio/Linkerd that handles it).","alternatives_considered":["No transport security on internal network — simpler but one network breach exposes everything","Shared secret tokens — weaker than mTLS, secrets can leak, no mutual verification"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Authentication mechanisms","evolution_path":"Auth Service abstracts auth mechanism from gateway. Can add OAuth2 authorization code flow, SAML, mutual TLS client certificates, or passkeys without touching gateway routing logic. Just add a new validation strategy to Auth Service."},{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Rate limit policies are stored as configuration (not hardcoded). New consumer tiers (e.g., premium partners at 5,000 req/min) can be added by creating a new policy and associating it with a consumer identity. No code changes needed."},{"area":"Webhook event types","evolution_path":"Webhook Dispatcher subscribes to event topics dynamically based on partner subscriptions. As new domain events are added to Logistics Core, partners can subscribe without changes to the dispatcher — just add the event type to the subscription."},{"area":"Gateway technology","evolution_path":"The architecture is technology-agnostic. Can start with Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway and migrate later. The key contracts (identity envelope, rate limit interface) are stable regardless of gateway implementation."},{"area":"Per-service authorization","evolution_path":"Authorization remains in downstream services, not the gateway. As authorization requirements grow more complex (RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC), each service can evolve its authorization model independently without gateway changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"X-Consumer-Identity header contract","reason":"All downstream services depend on this header format for identity. Changing its schema is a coordinated migration across every service. Must be versioned from day one."},{"area":"Network isolation boundary","reason":"Internal services must never be reachable from the public internet. This is a hard infrastructure constraint enforced at the network layer. Relaxing it would undermine the entire security model."},{"area":"Auth Service availability","reason":"Auth Service is on the critical path for every external request. It must maintain 99.99%+ availability. Cannot be casually refactored or merged into another service without careful analysis."},{"area":"Redis dependency for rate limiting","reason":"While fail-open mitigates outages, the rate limiting accuracy depends on Redis. Switching to a different backing store requires re-implementing the sliding window algorithm."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Auth Service becomes a single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy Auth Service as a multi-AZ, auto-scaled cluster. Gateway caches successful auth results for 60 seconds (tunable). If Auth Service is unreachable AND cache is empty, return 503 — never fail open on authentication (unlike rate limiting). Health checks with automatic instance replacement."},{"risk":"Gateway misconfiguration exposes internal services externally","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Infrastructure-as-code for all gateway routes with mandatory PR review. Integration tests that verify internal-only routes reject external traffic. Network-level enforcement (security groups) as a second layer — even if gateway routes are misconfigured, internal services are unreachable from outside the VPC."},{"risk":"Rate limit Redis outage allows traffic flood to downstream services","severity":"high","mitigation":"Fail-open is acceptable for short outages. For extended outages, gateway falls back to conservative per-instance local rate limiting (approximate but better than nothing). Alert on Redis health. Consider Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery failures cause partner data gaps","severity":"high","mitigation":"Dead letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Partner-facing dashboard showing delivery status. Manual retry endpoint for operations. Alert when delivery failure rate exceeds threshold. Store events for 7 days to allow replay."},{"risk":"Migration period with mixed auth (some services behind gateway, some not) creates inconsistency","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strangler fig approach with clear migration order. During transition, maintain a registry of which services are behind the gateway. Set a deadline for full migration. Services already behind the gateway reject direct external access immediately."},{"risk":"Identity header spoofing — external request injects X-Consumer-Identity header","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Gateway MUST strip X-Consumer-Identity from all incoming external requests before processing. Downstream services must verify requests come from internal network (source IP or mTLS) before trusting the header. Defense in depth at both layers."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): Deploy API Gateway with passthrough routing — no auth, no rate limiting. Verify all traffic flows correctly through the new gateway. Phase 2 (Week 3-4): Deploy Auth Service and integrate with gateway for mobile JWT validation only. Migrate one low-risk service to gateway-based auth. Phase 3 (Week 5-6): Add API key validation for partners. Add rate limiting with Redis. Migrate remaining services. Phase 4 (Week 7-8): Deploy Webhook Dispatcher. Set up Internal Gateway with mTLS. Remove auth/rate-limiting code from individual services. Each phase is independently deployable and rollback-safe.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/api-gateway/ — Gateway deployment config (Kong/Envoy declarative config or Terraform for managed gateway)","services/auth-service/ — New service: token validation, API key management, identity envelope schema","services/auth-service/proto/identity.proto — gRPC schema for ValidateToken RPC","lib/identity-envelope/ — Shared library defining X-Consumer-Identity header schema (used by all downstream services)","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — New service: subscription management, event consumption, delivery with retry","infra/redis/ — Redis cluster deployment for rate limiting","infra/network-policies/ — Kubernetes NetworkPolicy or security group rules isolating internal services","gateway/policies/mobile.yaml — Rate limit and auth policy for mobile routes","gateway/policies/partner.yaml — Rate limit and auth policy for partner routes","gateway/policies/internal.yaml — Internal-only route policy (reject external origin)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the gateway as a pure infrastructure concern — no business logic. Identity envelope should be a well-documented, versioned schema. All downstream services should use a shared middleware/library to parse the X-Consumer-Identity header, ensuring consistent behavior. Rate limit keys should follow the pattern {consumer_type}:{principal_id} for clean Redis key management. Webhook signatures should use HMAC-SHA256 with per-partner secrets.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put authorization logic in the gateway — authentication (who are you?) belongs in the gateway, authorization (can you do this?) belongs in the service that owns the resource","Do NOT hardcode rate limits — make them configurable per consumer identity, not per route","Do NOT skip header stripping — if the gateway doesn't strip X-Consumer-Identity from incoming requests, attackers can spoof identity","Do NOT make the gateway a transformation layer — keep request/response transformation minimal. If you need significant payload transformation, that is a BFF service, not a gateway concern","Do NOT deploy Auth Service and gateway simultaneously on day one — use the phased approach to reduce blast radius","Do NOT share Redis between rate limiting and application caching — use a dedicated Redis instance/cluster for rate limiting to isolate failure domains"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Gateway pattern selection — single deployment with route-based policies is well-proven at this scale","Auth centralization — clear win over inconsistent per-service implementations","Rate limiting approach — sliding window with Redis is industry standard","Network isolation for internal services — straightforward infrastructure concern","Phased migration strategy — strangler fig is the lowest-risk approach"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-managed) — depends on team expertise and infrastructure preferences not specified","Redis cluster sizing for rate limiting — depends on traffic volume not specified","Whether internal gateway should be a service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) or a simpler reverse proxy — depends on existing infrastructure"],"assumptions":["The platform runs in a cloud environment with VPC/network isolation capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a single gateway cluster can handle all consumer types","The team has or can acquire Kubernetes or equivalent orchestration for deploying the gateway","Downstream services can be modified to read X-Consumer-Identity headers (no black-box legacy services)","Redis operational expertise exists or can be acquired on the team"]}}

The design introduces a tiered gateway architecture with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal) sharing a common gateway core but with channel-specific policies. This eliminates duplicated auth logic from downstream services, centralizes rate limiting, and enforces network-level isolation for internal traffic. The architecture uses a single

content.text: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a tiered gateway architecture with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal) sharing a common gateway core but with channel-specific policies. This eliminates duplicated auth logic from downstream services, centralizes rate limiting, and enforces network-level isolation for internal traffic. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with route-based policy selection rather than three separate gateways, balancing operational simplicity against clean separation of concerns.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic (mobile + partner). Handles TLS termination, request routing, authentication dispatch, rate limiting enforcement, request/response transformation, and observability (access logs, metrics, tracing headers). Runs as a horizontally-scaled stateless cluster behind a load balancer.","boundaries":"Owns no business logic. Never calls databases directly. Only concerns: identity verification, policy enforcement, routing, and observability. Delegates auth token validation to the Auth Service."},{"name":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","responsibility":"Lightweight sidecar or internal-only ingress that routes service-to-service traffic. No authentication but enforces mTLS for transport security, provides circuit breaking, retries, and observability. Listens on a private network interface only.","boundaries":"Accessible only from the internal VPC/network. No public IP. No external DNS. Enforced via network policy (security groups or Kubernetes NetworkPolicy)."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Centralized authentication and identity resolution. Validates JWT tokens (mobile), validates API keys (partner), and returns a canonical identity envelope (principal ID, consumer type, scopes/permissions). Stateless validation with a shared signing key or JWKS endpoint.","boundaries":"Does not authorize — only authenticates and enriches. Authorization decisions remain with downstream services that understand their own domain rules."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Enforces per-consumer rate limits using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) on every response. Supports tiered limits: 100 req/min per mobile user, 1000 req/min per partner API key.","boundaries":"Shared Redis cluster for distributed counting. Gateway calls rate limiter as a pre-route filter. If Redis is unavailable, fails open with degraded logging (availability over correctness for rate limiting)."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages partner webhook subscriptions, serializes domain events into webhook payloads, delivers with exponential backoff retry (max 5 attempts), and tracks delivery status. Partners register endpoints via the Partner API.","boundaries":"Consumes domain events from an internal message broker (e.g., SQS, Kafka). Does not poll services. Isolated from the request path — purely async outbound."},{"name":"Downstream Services","responsibility":"Business logic services (orders, tracking, inventory, etc.). After gateway migration, these services no longer handle auth or rate limiting. They receive a trusted X-Consumer-Identity header from the gateway and make local authorization decisions.","boundaries":"Trust the gateway's identity header only when the request arrives from the internal network. Must reject requests with identity headers that arrive on any external interface (defense in depth)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT token in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry via Auth Service, extracts user_id, enforces 100 req/min rate limit keyed on user_id, then proxies to downstream with X-Consumer-Identity header.","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (HTTPS/REST)"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with API key in X-API-Key header. Gateway validates key via Auth Service, resolves partner_id and scopes, enforces 1000 req/min rate limit keyed on partner_id, then proxies to downstream with X-Consumer-Identity header including scopes.","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (HTTPS/REST)"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","contract":"mTLS over private network. No auth token required. Service identity derived from mTLS certificate CN. Internal gateway adds X-Consumer-Identity with service name and type=internal.","coupling_type":"platform coupling (network + mTLS)"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"gRPC call: ValidateToken(token, type) → IdentityEnvelope{principal_id, consumer_type, scopes[], expiry}. Sub-5ms p99 latency target. Gateway caches successful validations for 60s keyed on token hash.","coupling_type":"contract coupling (gRPC schema)"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"In-process filter or sidecar call: CheckRate(principal_id, consumer_type) → {allowed: bool, remaining: int, reset_at: timestamp}. Redis-backed sliding window.","coupling_type":"operational coupling (shared Redis)"},{"from":"Downstream Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker topic (e.g., order.shipped, tracking.updated). Webhook Dispatcher consumes events matching partner subscriptions and delivers via HTTPS POST with HMAC signature.","coupling_type":"event coupling (async, message broker)"}],"data_flow":"1. External request hits load balancer → API Gateway (Edge). 2. Gateway extracts credentials (JWT or API key) and calls Auth Service for validation. 3. On auth success, gateway calls Rate Limiter with resolved principal. 4. If under limit, gateway attaches X-Consumer-Identity header and proxies to downstream service. 5. Downstream service reads identity header, performs domain-level authorization, processes request, returns response. 6. Gateway strips internal headers from response, adds rate limit headers, returns to client. 7. For webhooks: downstream services emit domain events → message broker → Webhook Dispatcher → partner endpoints. 8. Internal service-to-service: requests go through Internal Gateway on private network with mTLS, bypassing Edge Gateway entirely.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) that were inconsistently duplicated across services. Single enforcement point eliminates the class of bugs where one service has weaker auth than another."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Services can be migrated incrementally. No big-bang rewrite required."},{"pattern":"Token Relay with Identity Propagation","rationale":"Gateway validates credentials and converts them into a canonical internal identity envelope. Can evolve auth mechanisms (add OAuth2, SAML) without redeploying gateway. Auth Service becomes a critical dependency — requires high availability.","alternatives_considered":["Embed JWT validation directly in gateway using JWKS — faster but couples gateway to auth mechanism, and API key validation still needs a service call","Sidecar auth proxy — adds deployment complexity without meaningful benefit over a shared service"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed sliding window rate limiter with fail-open policy","gains":"Accurate distributed rate limiting across gateway instances. Fail-open preserves availability during Redis outages — rate limiting is a protective measure, not a security boundary.","sacrifices":"During Redis outages, rate limits are not enforced. Malicious actors could exploit the window. Redis adds an infrastructure dependency.","alternatives_considered":["Local in-memory rate limiting per gateway instance — simpler but inaccurate when scaled horizontally (each instance sees only its own traffic)","Fail-closed rate limiting — safer but a Redis outage would cause a full platform outage, unacceptable for a logistics platform"]},{"decision":"mTLS for internal gateway instead of no security","gains":"Defense in depth. Even if network isolation is breached, services cannot be impersonated. Service identity is cryptographically verified.","sacrifices":"Certificate management overhead. Requires PKI infrastructure (or a service mesh like Istio/Linkerd that handles it).","alternatives_considered":["No transport security on internal network — simpler but one network breach exposes everything","Shared secret tokens — weaker than mTLS, secrets can leak, no mutual verification"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Authentication mechanisms","evolution_path":"Auth Service abstracts auth mechanism from gateway. Can add OAuth2 authorization code flow, SAML, mutual TLS client certificates, or passkeys without touching gateway routing logic. Just add a new validation strategy to Auth Service."},{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Rate limit policies are stored as configuration (not hardcoded). New consumer tiers (e.g., premium partners at 5,000 req/min) can be added by creating a new policy and associating it with a consumer identity. No code changes needed."},{"area":"Webhook event types","evolution_path":"Webhook Dispatcher subscribes to event topics dynamically based on partner subscriptions. As new domain events are added to Logistics Core, partners can subscribe without changes to the dispatcher — just add the event type to the subscription."},{"area":"Gateway technology","evolution_path":"The architecture is technology-agnostic. Can start with Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway and migrate later. The key contracts (identity envelope, rate limit interface) are stable regardless of gateway implementation."},{"area":"Per-service authorization","evolution_path":"Authorization remains in downstream services, not the gateway. As authorization requirements grow more complex (RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC), each service can evolve its authorization model independently without gateway changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"X-Consumer-Identity header contract","reason":"All downstream services depend on this header format for identity. Changing its schema is a coordinated migration across every service. Must be versioned from day one."},{"area":"Network isolation boundary","reason":"Internal services must never be reachable from the public internet. This is a hard infrastructure constraint enforced at the network layer. Relaxing it would undermine the entire security model."},{"area":"Auth Service availability","reason":"Auth Service is on the critical path for every external request. It must maintain 99.99%+ availability. Cannot be casually refactored or merged into another service without careful analysis."},{"area":"Redis dependency for rate limiting","reason":"While fail-open mitigates outages, the rate limiting accuracy depends on Redis. Switching to a different backing store requires re-implementing the sliding window algorithm."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Auth Service becomes a single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy Auth Service as a multi-AZ, auto-scaled cluster. Gateway caches successful auth results for 60 seconds (tunable). If Auth Service is unreachable AND cache is empty, return 503 — never fail open on authentication (unlike rate limiting). Health checks with automatic instance replacement."},{"risk":"Gateway misconfiguration exposes internal services externally","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Infrastructure-as-code for all gateway routes with mandatory PR review. Integration tests that verify internal-only routes reject external traffic. Network-level enforcement (security groups) as a second layer — even if gateway routes are misconfigured, internal services are unreachable from outside the VPC."},{"risk":"Rate limit Redis outage allows traffic flood to downstream services","severity":"high","mitigation":"Fail-open is acceptable for short outages. For extended outages, gateway falls back to conservative per-instance local rate limiting (approximate but better than nothing). Alert on Redis health. Consider Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery failures cause partner data gaps","severity":"high","mitigation":"Dead letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Partner-facing dashboard showing delivery status. Manual retry endpoint for operations. Alert when delivery failure rate exceeds threshold. Store events for 7 days to allow replay."},{"risk":"Migration period with mixed auth (some services behind gateway, some not) creates inconsistency","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strangler fig approach with clear migration order. During transition, maintain a registry of which services are behind the gateway. Set a deadline for full migration. Services already behind the gateway reject direct external access immediately."},{"risk":"Identity header spoofing — external request injects X-Consumer-Identity header","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Gateway MUST strip X-Consumer-Identity from all incoming external requests before processing. Downstream services must verify requests come from internal network (source IP or mTLS) before trusting the header. Defense in depth at both layers."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): Deploy API Gateway with passthrough routing — no auth, no rate limiting. Verify all traffic flows correctly through the new gateway. Phase 2 (Week 3-4): Deploy Auth Service and integrate with gateway for mobile JWT validation only. Migrate one low-risk service to gateway-based auth. Phase 3 (Week 5-6): Add API key validation for partners. Add rate limiting with Redis. Migrate remaining services. Phase 4 (Week 7-8): Deploy Webhook Dispatcher. Set up Internal Gateway with mTLS. Remove auth/rate-limiting code from individual services. Each phase is independently deployable and rollback-safe.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/api-gateway/ — Gateway deployment config (Kong/Envoy declarative config or Terraform for managed gateway)","services/auth-service/ — New service: token validation, API key management, identity envelope schema","services/auth-service/proto/identity.proto — gRPC schema for ValidateToken RPC","lib/identity-envelope/ — Shared library defining X-Consumer-Identity header schema (used by all downstream services)","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — New service: subscription management, event consumption, delivery with retry","infra/redis/ — Redis cluster deployment for rate limiting","infra/network-policies/ — Kubernetes NetworkPolicy or security group rules isolating internal services","gateway/policies/mobile.yaml — Rate limit and auth policy for mobile routes","gateway/policies/partner.yaml — Rate limit and auth policy for partner routes","gateway/policies/internal.yaml — Internal-only route policy (reject external origin)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the gateway as a pure infrastructure concern — no business logic. Identity envelope should be a well-documented, versioned schema. All downstream services should use a shared middleware/library to parse the X-Consumer-Identity header, ensuring consistent behavior. Rate limit keys should follow the pattern {consumer_type}:{principal_id} for clean Redis key management. Webhook signatures should use HMAC-SHA256 with per-partner secrets.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put authorization logic in the gateway — authentication (who are you?) belongs in the gateway, authorization (can you do this?) belongs in the service that owns the resource","Do NOT hardcode rate limits — make them configurable per consumer identity, not per route","Do NOT skip header stripping — if the gateway doesn't strip X-Consumer-Identity from incoming requests, attackers can spoof identity","Do NOT make the gateway a transformation layer — keep request/response transformation minimal. If you need significant payload transformation, that is a BFF service, not a gateway concern","Do NOT deploy Auth Service and gateway simultaneously on day one — use the phased approach to reduce blast radius","Do NOT share Redis between rate limiting and application caching — use a dedicated Redis instance/cluster for rate limiting to isolate failure domains"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Gateway pattern selection — single deployment with route-based policies is well-proven at this scale","Auth centralization — clear win over inconsistent per-service implementations","Rate limiting approach — sliding window with Redis is industry standard","Network isolation for internal services — straightforward infrastructure concern","Phased migration strategy — strangler fig is the lowest-risk approach"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-managed) — depends on team expertise and infrastructure preferences not specified","Redis cluster sizing for rate limiting — depends on traffic volume not specified","Whether internal gateway should be a service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) or a simpler reverse proxy — depends on existing infrastructure"],"assumptions":["The platform runs in a cloud environment with VPC/network isolation capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a single gateway cluster can handle all consumer types","The team has or can acquire Kubernetes or equivalent orchestration for deploying the gateway","Downstream services can be modified to read X-Consumer-Identity headers (no black-box legacy services)","Redis operational expertise exists or can be acquired on the team"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a tiered gateway architecture with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal) sharing a common gateway core but with channel-specific policies. This eliminates duplicated auth logic from downstream services, centralizes rate limiting, and enforces network-level isolation for internal traffic. The architecture uses a single gateway deployment with route-based policy selection rather than three separate gateways, balancing operational simplicity against clean separation of concerns.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic (mobile + partner). Handles TLS termination, request routing, authentication dispatch, rate limiting enforcement, request/response transformation, and observability (access logs, metrics, tracing headers). Runs as a horizontally-scaled stateless cluster behind a load balancer.","boundaries":"Owns no business logic. Never calls databases directly. Only concerns: identity verification, policy enforcement, routing, and observability. Delegates auth token validation to the Auth Service."},{"name":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","responsibility":"Lightweight sidecar or internal-only ingress that routes service-to-service traffic. No authentication but enforces mTLS for transport security, provides circuit breaking, retries, and observability. Listens on a private network interface only.","boundaries":"Accessible only from the internal VPC/network. No public IP. No external DNS. Enforced via network policy (security groups or Kubernetes NetworkPolicy)."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Centralized authentication and identity resolution. Validates JWT tokens (mobile), validates API keys (partner), and returns a canonical identity envelope (principal ID, consumer type, scopes/permissions). Stateless validation with a shared signing key or JWKS endpoint.","boundaries":"Does not authorize — only authenticates and enriches. Authorization decisions remain with downstream services that understand their own domain rules."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Enforces per-consumer rate limits using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) on every response. Supports tiered limits: 100 req/min per mobile user, 1000 req/min per partner API key.","boundaries":"Shared Redis cluster for distributed counting. Gateway calls rate limiter as a pre-route filter. If Redis is unavailable, fails open with degraded logging (availability over correctness for rate limiting)."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages partner webhook subscriptions, serializes domain events into webhook payloads, delivers with exponential backoff retry (max 5 attempts), and tracks delivery status. Partners register endpoints via the Partner API.","boundaries":"Consumes domain events from an internal message broker (e.g., SQS, Kafka). Does not poll services. Isolated from the request path — purely async outbound."},{"name":"Downstream Services","responsibility":"Business logic services (orders, tracking, inventory, etc.). After gateway migration, these services no longer handle auth or rate limiting. They receive a trusted X-Consumer-Identity header from the gateway and make local authorization decisions.","boundaries":"Trust the gateway's identity header only when the request arrives from the internal network. Must reject requests with identity headers that arrive on any external interface (defense in depth)."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with Bearer JWT token in Authorization header. Gateway validates JWT signature and expiry via Auth Service, extracts user_id, enforces 100 req/min rate limit keyed on user_id, then proxies to downstream with X-Consumer-Identity header.","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (HTTPS/REST)"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS with API key in X-API-Key header. Gateway validates key via Auth Service, resolves partner_id and scopes, enforces 1000 req/min rate limit keyed on partner_id, then proxies to downstream with X-Consumer-Identity header including scopes.","coupling_type":"protocol coupling (HTTPS/REST)"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Gateway (Mesh)","contract":"mTLS over private network. No auth token required. Service identity derived from mTLS certificate CN. Internal gateway adds X-Consumer-Identity with service name and type=internal.","coupling_type":"platform coupling (network + mTLS)"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"gRPC call: ValidateToken(token, type) → IdentityEnvelope{principal_id, consumer_type, scopes[], expiry}. Sub-5ms p99 latency target. Gateway caches successful validations for 60s keyed on token hash.","coupling_type":"contract coupling (gRPC schema)"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"In-process filter or sidecar call: CheckRate(principal_id, consumer_type) → {allowed: bool, remaining: int, reset_at: timestamp}. Redis-backed sliding window.","coupling_type":"operational coupling (shared Redis)"},{"from":"Downstream Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to message broker topic (e.g., order.shipped, tracking.updated). Webhook Dispatcher consumes events matching partner subscriptions and delivers via HTTPS POST with HMAC signature.","coupling_type":"event coupling (async, message broker)"}],"data_flow":"1. External request hits load balancer → API Gateway (Edge). 2. Gateway extracts credentials (JWT or API key) and calls Auth Service for validation. 3. On auth success, gateway calls Rate Limiter with resolved principal. 4. If under limit, gateway attaches X-Consumer-Identity header and proxies to downstream service. 5. Downstream service reads identity header, performs domain-level authorization, processes request, returns response. 6. Gateway strips internal headers from response, adds rate limit headers, returns to client. 7. For webhooks: downstream services emit domain events → message broker → Webhook Dispatcher → partner endpoints. 8. Internal service-to-service: requests go through Internal Gateway on private network with mTLS, bypassing Edge Gateway entirely.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, logging) that were inconsistently duplicated across services. Single enforcement point eliminates the class of bugs where one service has weaker auth than another."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Services can be migrated incrementally. Gateway routes to existing services unchanged — the only difference is auth/rate-limiting moves to the gateway. No big-bang rewrite required."},{"pattern":"Token Relay with Identity Propagation","rationale":"Gateway validates credentials and converts them into a canonical internal identity envelope. Downstream services never see raw tokens or API keys — they get a trusted, normalized identity. This decouples internal services from auth mechanism changes."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed-window counters (avoids burst at window boundaries). Redis-backed for distributed counting across gateway instances. Fails open to preserve availability."},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker (Internal Gateway)","rationale":"Prevents cascade failures in service-to-service communication. Internal gateway applies circuit breaking per upstream service."},{"pattern":"Async Webhook Delivery with Retry","rationale":"Decouples webhook delivery from the request path. Exponential backoff prevents thundering herd on partner endpoint recovery."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"API Access Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["ConsumerIdentity (entity: principal_id, type, scopes, rate_limit_tier)","APIKey (entity: key_hash, partner_id, scopes, active, created_at)","RateLimitPolicy (value object: tier, max_requests, window_seconds)"]},{"name":"Webhook Delivery","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription (entity: partner_id, event_types[], endpoint_url, secret, active)","DeliveryAttempt (entity: subscription_id, event_id, status, attempts, next_retry_at)"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Tracking — these are the actual business domain aggregates, unchanged by gateway introduction"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Delivery","relationship":"Customer-Supplier: Logistics Core publishes domain events. Webhook Delivery subscribes and transforms them into partner-facing payloads. Logistics Core does not know about webhooks."},{"upstream":"API Access Management","downstream":"API Gateway (Edge)","relationship":"Open Host Service: Auth Service exposes a gRPC API for identity validation. Gateway is a consumer. Auth Service defines the contract."},{"upstream":"API Access Management","downstream":"Webhook Delivery","relationship":"Conformist: Webhook Delivery uses partner_id from API Access Management's identity model to match subscriptions. It conforms to the identity model without translating."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single gateway deployment with route-based policy selection instead of separate gateways per consumer type","gains":"Lower operational overhead — one deployment to monitor, scale, and patch. Shared infrastructure cost. Simpler DNS and certificate management.","sacrifices":"Blast radius: a gateway misconfiguration or overload affects all consumer types simultaneously. Policy complexity lives in route configuration rather than deployment topology.","alternatives_considered":["Three separate gateway deployments (one per consumer type) — cleaner isolation but 3x operational burden and infrastructure cost","BFF pattern with separate backends — over-engineered for current scale, better suited when mobile and partner APIs diverge significantly in shape"]},{"decision":"Centralized Auth Service rather than gateway-embedded auth logic","gains":"Auth logic is testable and deployable independently. Can evolve auth mechanisms (add OAuth2, SAML) without redeploying gateway. Single source of truth for key/token validation.","sacrifices":"Added network hop for every request (mitigated by 60s cache). Auth Service becomes a critical dependency — requires high availability.","alternatives_considered":["Embed JWT validation directly in gateway using JWKS — faster but couples gateway to auth mechanism, and API key validation still needs a service call","Sidecar auth proxy — adds deployment complexity without meaningful benefit over a shared service"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed sliding window rate limiter with fail-open policy","gains":"Accurate distributed rate limiting across gateway instances. Fail-open preserves availability during Redis outages — rate limiting is a protective measure, not a security boundary.","sacrifices":"During Redis outages, rate limits are not enforced. Malicious actors could exploit the window. Redis adds an infrastructure dependency.","alternatives_considered":["Local in-memory rate limiting per gateway instance — simpler but inaccurate when scaled horizontally (each instance sees only its own traffic)","Fail-closed rate limiting — safer but a Redis outage would cause a full platform outage, unacceptable for a logistics platform"]},{"decision":"mTLS for internal gateway instead of no security","gains":"Defense in depth. Even if network isolation is breached, services cannot be impersonated. Service identity is cryptographically verified.","sacrifices":"Certificate management overhead. Requires PKI infrastructure (or a service mesh like Istio/Linkerd that handles it).","alternatives_considered":["No transport security on internal network — simpler but one network breach exposes everything","Shared secret tokens — weaker than mTLS, secrets can leak, no mutual verification"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Authentication mechanisms","evolution_path":"Auth Service abstracts auth mechanism from gateway. Can add OAuth2 authorization code flow, SAML, mutual TLS client certificates, or passkeys without touching gateway routing logic. Just add a new validation strategy to Auth Service."},{"area":"Rate limit tiers","evolution_path":"Rate limit policies are stored as configuration (not hardcoded). New consumer tiers (e.g., premium partners at 5,000 req/min) can be added by creating a new policy and associating it with a consumer identity. No code changes needed."},{"area":"Webhook event types","evolution_path":"Webhook Dispatcher subscribes to event topics dynamically based on partner subscriptions. As new domain events are added to Logistics Core, partners can subscribe without changes to the dispatcher — just add the event type to the subscription."},{"area":"Gateway technology","evolution_path":"The architecture is technology-agnostic. Can start with Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway and migrate later. The key contracts (identity envelope, rate limit interface) are stable regardless of gateway implementation."},{"area":"Per-service authorization","evolution_path":"Authorization remains in downstream services, not the gateway. As authorization requirements grow more complex (RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC), each service can evolve its authorization model independently without gateway changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"X-Consumer-Identity header contract","reason":"All downstream services depend on this header format for identity. Changing its schema is a coordinated migration across every service. Must be versioned from day one."},{"area":"Network isolation boundary","reason":"Internal services must never be reachable from the public internet. This is a hard infrastructure constraint enforced at the network layer. Relaxing it would undermine the entire security model."},{"area":"Auth Service availability","reason":"Auth Service is on the critical path for every external request. It must maintain 99.99%+ availability. Cannot be casually refactored or merged into another service without careful analysis."},{"area":"Redis dependency for rate limiting","reason":"While fail-open mitigates outages, the rate limiting accuracy depends on Redis. Switching to a different backing store requires re-implementing the sliding window algorithm."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Auth Service becomes a single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy Auth Service as a multi-AZ, auto-scaled cluster. Gateway caches successful auth results for 60 seconds (tunable). If Auth Service is unreachable AND cache is empty, return 503 — never fail open on authentication (unlike rate limiting). Health checks with automatic instance replacement."},{"risk":"Gateway misconfiguration exposes internal services externally","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Infrastructure-as-code for all gateway routes with mandatory PR review. Integration tests that verify internal-only routes reject external traffic. Network-level enforcement (security groups) as a second layer — even if gateway routes are misconfigured, internal services are unreachable from outside the VPC."},{"risk":"Rate limit Redis outage allows traffic flood to downstream services","severity":"high","mitigation":"Fail-open is acceptable for short outages. For extended outages, gateway falls back to conservative per-instance local rate limiting (approximate but better than nothing). Alert on Redis health. Consider Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA."},{"risk":"Webhook delivery failures cause partner data gaps","severity":"high","mitigation":"Dead letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Partner-facing dashboard showing delivery status. Manual retry endpoint for operations. Alert when delivery failure rate exceeds threshold. Store events for 7 days to allow replay."},{"risk":"Migration period with mixed auth (some services behind gateway, some not) creates inconsistency","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strangler fig approach with clear migration order. During transition, maintain a registry of which services are behind the gateway. Set a deadline for full migration. Services already behind the gateway reject direct external access immediately."},{"risk":"Identity header spoofing — external request injects X-Consumer-Identity header","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Gateway MUST strip X-Consumer-Identity from all incoming external requests before processing. Downstream services must verify requests come from internal network (source IP or mTLS) before trusting the header. Defense in depth at both layers."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Week 1-2): Deploy API Gateway with passthrough routing — no auth, no rate limiting. Verify all traffic flows correctly through the new gateway. Phase 2 (Week 3-4): Deploy Auth Service and integrate with gateway for mobile JWT validation only. Migrate one low-risk service to gateway-based auth. Phase 3 (Week 5-6): Add API key validation for partners. Add rate limiting with Redis. Migrate remaining services. Phase 4 (Week 7-8): Deploy Webhook Dispatcher. Set up Internal Gateway with mTLS. Remove auth/rate-limiting code from individual services. Each phase is independently deployable and rollback-safe.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/api-gateway/ — Gateway deployment config (Kong/Envoy declarative config or Terraform for managed gateway)","services/auth-service/ — New service: token validation, API key management, identity envelope schema","services/auth-service/proto/identity.proto — gRPC schema for ValidateToken RPC","lib/identity-envelope/ — Shared library defining X-Consumer-Identity header schema (used by all downstream services)","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — New service: subscription management, event consumption, delivery with retry","infra/redis/ — Redis cluster deployment for rate limiting","infra/network-policies/ — Kubernetes NetworkPolicy or security group rules isolating internal services","gateway/policies/mobile.yaml — Rate limit and auth policy for mobile routes","gateway/policies/partner.yaml — Rate limit and auth policy for partner routes","gateway/policies/internal.yaml — Internal-only route policy (reject external origin)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the gateway as a pure infrastructure concern — no business logic. Identity envelope should be a well-documented, versioned schema. All downstream services should use a shared middleware/library to parse the X-Consumer-Identity header, ensuring consistent behavior. Rate limit keys should follow the pattern {consumer_type}:{principal_id} for clean Redis key management. Webhook signatures should use HMAC-SHA256 with per-partner secrets.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put authorization logic in the gateway — authentication (who are you?) belongs in the gateway, authorization (can you do this?) belongs in the service that owns the resource","Do NOT hardcode rate limits — make them configurable per consumer identity, not per route","Do NOT skip header stripping — if the gateway doesn't strip X-Consumer-Identity from incoming requests, attackers can spoof identity","Do NOT make the gateway a transformation layer — keep request/response transformation minimal. If you need significant payload transformation, that is a BFF service, not a gateway concern","Do NOT deploy Auth Service and gateway simultaneously on day one — use the phased approach to reduce blast radius","Do NOT share Redis between rate limiting and application caching — use a dedicated Redis instance/cluster for rate limiting to isolate failure domains"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Gateway pattern selection — single deployment with route-based policies is well-proven at this scale","Auth centralization — clear win over inconsistent per-service implementations","Rate limiting approach — sliding window with Redis is industry standard","Network isolation for internal services — straightforward infrastructure concern","Phased migration strategy — strangler fig is the lowest-risk approach"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-managed) — depends on team expertise and infrastructure preferences not specified","Redis cluster sizing for rate limiting — depends on traffic volume not specified","Whether internal gateway should be a service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) or a simpler reverse proxy — depends on existing infrastructure"],"assumptions":["The platform runs in a cloud environment with VPC/network isolation capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate enough that a single gateway cluster can handle all consumer types","The team has or can acquire Kubernetes or equivalent orchestration for deploying the gateway","Downstream services can be modified to read X-Consumer-Identity headers (no black-box legacy services)","Redis operational expertise exists or can be acquired on the team"]}}
